1: gibbôr ḥayil wᵉhûʾ mᵉṣōrāʿ — "a mighty man of valor — and he had leprosy." The syntax places the military honorifics first (H1368 gibbôr, mighty warrior; H2428 ḥayil, valor/force) and the disease last, as a devastating qualifier: all the greatness ends with wᵉhûʾ mᵉṣōrāʿ ("but he was a leper"). The word ṣāraʿat (H6879) covers a range of skin diseases, not necessarily what modern medicine calls leprosy (Hansen's disease); it is the condition that rendered one ritually impure and excluded from community (Lev 13-14). The irony is the entire verse's point: the man through whom YHWH had given victory to Aram is himself unable to receive victory over his own flesh.
2: naʿărāh qᵉṭannāh — "a little girl." The double diminutive (naʿărāh, H5291 — young girl; qāṭān, H6996 — small, young) emphasizes her insignificance in social terms: a war captive, a household servant of a foreign general's wife. She has no name, no identity beyond her age and captive status. She is the unexpected source of the healing information — the paradigm of the lowly, unnamed instrument through whom divine provision flows. Her confidence — "he would cure him" — is stated in the conditional imperfect: yēʾāsēp (H622, gather/remove/take away), more literally "he would remove it from him."
3: The girl's speech uses the optative lûʾ (H3863 — would that/if only) + imperfect: "would that my lord were before the prophet." The conditional structure (ʾāz yēʾāsēp, "then he would remove") expresses confident hope, not doubt. mᵉṣōrāʿtô — "his leprosy" uses the Pual participle noun; the word becomes the organizing term of the entire chapter (occurs 7 times in 2 Kings 5).
4: The chain of transmission — girl to mistress to Naaman to king — illustrates how divine initiative enters human political structures from the margins. The verb nāgad (H5046 — tell, report, declare) drives the narrative forward: it is used for both the girl's speech to the wife and Naaman's speech to the king. Information that originates with a captive child becomes a royal diplomatic initiative within two verses.
5: ʾiggeret ʾel melek yiśrāʾēl — "a letter to the king of Israel." The king of Aram reduces the prophetic healing to a royal transaction: šālaḥ sēper (send a letter). The gifts Naaman carries — ten talents of silver (kikkārîm), six thousand shekels of gold (šeqalîm), and ten changes of clothing (ḥᵃlîpōt bᵉgāḏîm) — are diplomatic tribute scaled to the request. The enormous wealth (roughly 10 talents = ~300 kg of silver; 6,000 shekels = ~70 kg of gold) reflects how much Naaman and the king of Aram are willing to spend.
6: The letter says wᵉʾāsaptā ʾet ṣāraʿtô — "you will remove/cure his leprosy." The same verb as v2 (ʾāsap, H622 — gather, take away). The letter addresses the king of Israel as if he is a healer — a fundamental category error the king immediately perceives (v7). The reduction of prophetic healing to royal transaction sets up the theological critique of the entire narrative.
7: hăʾĕlōhîm ʾānî lᵉhāmît ûlᵉhaḥᵃyôt — "Am I God, to kill and to make alive?" The king's theological insight is correct: the formulation māmît ûmᵉḥayyeh (H4191 + H2421 — kill and give life) is the exact language of Deuteronomy 32:39 ("I kill and I make alive") and 1 Samuel 2:6 (Hannah's song). The king identifies the request as requiring divine power — he just fails to see that YHWH works through his prophet Elisha. His tearing of clothes (wayyiqraʿ bᵉgāḏāyw, H7167) signals the depth of perceived threat: this looks like a pretext for war.
8: yāḏaʿ kî yēš nāḇîʾ bᵉyiśrāʾēl — "let him know that there is a prophet in Israel." Elisha's intervention reframes the entire situation: the king's role is to redirect Naaman to the prophet, not to perform the healing himself. yāḏaʿ (H3045 — know) in the imperfect + kî frames the purpose of the prophetic demonstration: Naaman will know experientially that a prophet exists in Israel. This knowing is the narrative's goal before the theological confession of v15.
9: Naaman arrives with his military equipment — sûsāyw wᵉrikibbô (his horses and his chariot). He stops at the door of Elisha's house: pethah bayit ʾĕlîšāʿ. The juxtaposition of military pomp and a house door is deliberate. The great man with six thousand shekels of gold stands at a prophet's door — the social inversion the narrative is building toward.
10: lēḵ wᵉrāḥaṣtā šeḇaʿ pᵉʿāmîm bayardēn — "Go and wash (rāḥaṣ, H7364) in the Jordan seven times." Elisha sends a messenger, not himself. Two linguistic points: (1) rāḥaṣ (wash, bathe) is the verb in the instruction; ṭāḇal (dip, immerse) is the verb in v14 for what Naaman actually does — the difference between "wash yourself" and "immerse yourself" is significant; (2) yardēn (Jordan) is the covenant-crossing river, first named in relation to Israel's entry into the land (Josh 3-4). The instruction localizes the healing in the specific geography of covenant.
11: ʾāmar ʾānî ʾāmarti — "I thought to myself." The doubled speech verb (ʾāmar + ʾānî ʾāmarti, H559) marks Naaman's angry internal monologue. He expected: Elisha to come out (yāṣōʾ yēṣēʾ, imperfect emphatic — "he would surely come out"), to stand before him (wᵉʿāmaḏ), to call on the name of YHWH, and to wave his hand (wᵉhēnîp yāḏô, H5130 — wave, brandish) over the affected place. The expected ritual is dramatic, public, and involves direct contact — everything Elisha's actual instruction lacks. Naaman's expectations are precisely wrong.
12: hᵃlōʾ ṭôḇ... mikkōl mê yiśrāʾēl — "Are not [these rivers] better than all the waters of Israel?" ṭôḇ (H2895 — good, better) is the same evaluative term used throughout Genesis for creation. Naaman's argument is logical on its own terms: the rivers of Damascus (Abana/Amana and Pharpar) are large, clear rivers — demonstrably "better" as rivers than the Jordan, which is muddy and unremarkable. His question uses the same verb rāḥaṣ (wash) that Elisha used: "could I not wash in them and be clean?" The answer the narrative gives is: yes, but not for Naaman, not now, not for this purpose.
13: ʾāḇî — "my father." Naaman's servants address him with the term of intimate respect normally used for a father or a highly regarded elder. Their logic is irrefutable: if Elisha had commanded something difficult (dāḇār gāḏôl — a great/difficult word), Naaman would have done it. raq (H7535 — only, merely, just) emphasizes the smallness of the actual request: "how much more should you comply when he merely says: wash and be clean." The raq identifies Naaman's resistance as inversely proportional to the simplicity of the command.
14: wayyiṭbōl bᵉyardēn šeḇaʿ pᵉʿāmîm — "he dipped himself in the Jordan seven times." The verb here is ṭāḇal (H2881 — to dip, immerse, plunge into), not rāḥaṣ (wash) that Elisha commanded. Naaman does not merely wash but immerses. wᵉyāšāḇ bᵉśārô — "his flesh was restored" uses šûḇ (H7725 — return, turn back, restore) in the Qal imperfect: the flesh literally "returned" or "turned back" — not just healed but restored to its original condition. The comparison — kᵉḇaśar naʿar qāṭōn, "like the flesh of a little child" — uses the same phrase (naʿar qāṭōn) as the servant girl of v2. The little girl and the restored little-child flesh bracket the narrative.
15: yāḏaʿtî kî ʾên ʾĕlōhîm bᵉḵol hāʾāreṣ kî ʾim bᵉyiśrāʾēl — "I know that there is no God in all the earth except in Israel." The verb yāḏaʿ (H3045 — know) is now in the perfect: Naaman knows, has come to know. His monotheistic confession uses kî ʾim (H3588 + H518 — except, only) — the exclusive statement. His offer of a bᵉrāḵāh (H1293 — blessing, gift) to Elisha uses the covenant word for blessing; the "blessing" offered is the gift brought from Damascus. He presents what he brought as a gift of gratitude, using the beautiful ambiguity of the word that means both divine blessing and a material gift.
16: ḥay YHWH ʾăšer ʿāmadtî lᵉpānāyw — "As YHWH lives, before whom I stand." Elisha's refusal is sworn by the same oath formula used by Elijah (1 Kings 17:1; 2 Kings 18:15). The phrase ʿāmadtî lᵉpānāyw (I stand before him) identifies Elisha as a courtier of YHWH — one who serves at the divine court. The refusal of the gift is theologically grounded: to accept payment would imply that the healing was a human service rather than a divine grace. wayyipṣar (H6484 — to urge strongly, press) — Naaman pressed him, but Elisha refused.
17: ʾădāmāh (H127 — ground, soil, earth) — two mule-loads of ʾădāmāh. The choice of ʾădāmāh rather than ʾereṣ (land, territory, H776) is significant: ʾădāmāh is the fertile, life-giving soil — the word of Genesis 2:7 where YHWH forms the human from the ʾădāmāh. Naaman wants Israel's covenant soil to stand on when he worships YHWH. His theological reasoning — "your servant will offer burnt offering and sacrifice to no other god but YHWH" — reflects the ancient assumption that gods inhabit specific territories; his request is to bring Israel's sacred ground into Aram so that his worship of YHWH has a legitimate substrate.
18: sᵉlaḥ nā lᵉʿaḇdᵉḵā — "pardon your servant." The verb sālaḥ (H5545 — forgive, pardon) is the specific word for divine forgiveness, used almost exclusively with YHWH as subject in the OT (Numbers 14:19-20; 1 Kings 8:30ff). Naaman directs this vocabulary to Elisha as mediator. The Rimmôn temple (H7417 — the Aramean storm god, cognate with Hadad) creates the political dilemma: Naaman must physically support the king when he bows in the pagan temple. His request — wᵉhištaḥᵃwîtî (and I bow down, H7812 — twice in this verse) — is honest about what he must do while declaring where his actual allegiance lies. The tension between bodily conformity and interior conviction is a genuine theological problem to which Elisha gives no rebuke.
19: lēḵ lᵉšālôm — "Go in peace." Elisha's send-off uses the standard Hebrew farewell formula but with the full weight of šālôm: wholeness, covenant completion. He makes no ruling on the Rimmon question; the silence is the answer. The brevity of his response — two words — after the theological complexity of vv17-18 is itself significant: the prophet does not legislate what Naaman must do in the Aramean court. The grace given is not conditional on perfect circumstances.
20: wayyōʾmer gēḥăzî naʿar ʾĕlîšāʿ — "Gehazi, the servant (naʿar) of Elisha, said to himself." The internal monologue (wᵉhûʾ nāʾamar, literally "and he said [to himself]") follows the pattern of Naaman's internal speech in v11. Gehazi's rationalization uses the same oath formula as Elisha in v16 (ḥay YHWH) — he swears by YHWH to commit a sin against YHWH's intent. rûṣ ʾaḥᵃrāyw (H7323 + H310 — run after him) — the verb of urgency that the Shunammite used in urgent faith (2 Kings 4:25) is here used in urgent greed.
21: Naaman sees Gehazi running after him (rāṣ ʾaḥᵃrāyw) and descends from his chariot — a sign of respect to Elisha's representative. The question hăšālôm — "is everything well?" — echoes the šālôm language of 2 Kings 4. His response to what he perceives as the prophet's servant in legitimate service is warm and immediate.
22: šālôm — Gehazi's use of the peace-formula is the opposite of the Shunammite's: she used it as a statement of faith in adverse circumstances; he uses it as a deceptive opener for a fraudulent request. The lie is elaborate: two sons of the prophets, hill country of Ephraim, a talent of silver and two changes of clothing. The request is modest (compared to Naaman's original gift) — which makes it more plausible. Gehazi uses Elisha's name and authority (ʾǎdōnî šᵉlāḥanî, "my master has sent me") falsely.
23: hôʾel qah šᵉnê kikkārîm — "please accept two talents." Naaman insists on more than asked — he doubles the amount, pressing (pāraṣ, H6555 — urge, press, insist) Gehazi to take two talents. He binds the silver in two bags and hands them to his own servants to carry before Gehazi. The generous Gentile gives twice what the fraudulent Israelite requested.
24: At the hill (hāʿōpel, H6077 — a steep mound, specifically used of the citadel area in Samaria; cf. 2 Kings 5:24 and Micah 1:5) Gehazi takes the goods from Naaman's servants and hides them in the house. The verb pāqad (H6485 — attend to, set in place) used here for storing the goods contrasts with the open exchange of the previous verses. The concealment is the basis for the lie to Elisha in v25.
25: ʾānāh hālaḵtā gēḥăzî — "Where did you go, Gehazi?" The question is a test — Elisha already knows (v26). Gehazi's denial — lōʾ hālaḵ ʿaḇdᵉḵā ʾānāh wᵉʾānāh, "your servant has not gone here or there" — uses the indefinite doubling of ʾānāh wᵉʾānāh (H575 × 2) for "anywhere at all." The absolute denial makes the lie complete.
26: lōʾ libbî hālaḵ ʾittᵉḵā — "Did not my heart go with you?" The noun lēḵ (from lāḵaḵ? No — this is lēḇ, H3820, heart/mind) — "was not my heart going with you?" — is a claim to prophetic awareness of Gehazi's journey. Elisha's heart (mind/perception) tracked Gehazi's movements in real time. The question hāʿēt lāqaḥat — "is this the time to take/receive?" — uses the ʿēt (H6256 — time, season, appointed time) of covenantal opportunity: there is a right time and a wrong time; this was the wrong time for a monetary transaction that would commercialize divine grace.
27: wᵉṣāraʿat naʿᵃmān tiḏbaq bᵉḵā — "the leprosy of Naaman will cling (dāḇaq, H1692) to you." The verb dāḇaq is the strongest Hebrew verb for adhesion, attachment, and covenant loyalty: it describes Ruth clinging to Naomi (Ruth 1:14), the covenant command to "hold fast" to YHWH (Deuteronomy 11:22; 2 Kings 13:5), and marital union (Genesis 2:24). The irony is complete: the verb that describes unbreakable covenant devotion to YHWH is now used for the unbreakable punishment that will attend Gehazi and his descendants forever (lᵉʿôlām). What should have clung to him (covenant loyalty) did not; what should not cling to him (Naaman's leprosy) now will — forever. Gehazi goes out from Elisha's presence leprous, "white as snow" (mᵉṣōrāʿ kaššāleg) — the same snow-white appearance as Moses's temporary leprous condition (Exodus 4:6) and Miriam's punishment (Numbers 12:10), but for Gehazi, permanent.
